stories.headline: Update: Iran Leadership Vacuum Deepens as New Supreme Leader Remains Unseen and Decision-Making Fractures
stories.summary:
New details have emerged about the crisis of authority inside Iran's government as the country's new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, has not been seen in public since assuming power following the killing of his father in February. The New York Times, citing Iranian sources, reported this week that he may have suffered multiple injuries in the initial strikes, including facial wounds that have made it difficult for him to speak. Iranian officials have acknowledged he was hurt but have offered few specifics, while the government distributed a mass text message to citizens Thursday insisting there was "no such thing as a hardliner or moderate in Iran — just one nation, one course."

In practice, observers say decision-making has become less centralized and harder to read. Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi continues to represent Tehran diplomatically, but a brief and quickly retracted statement suggesting the Strait of Hormuz had reopened highlighted how little coordination exists between Iran's diplomatic and military tracks. Parliament Speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf is formally heading Iran's negotiating delegation, further muddying lines of authority.

President Trump described Iran's leadership as "fractured" and said the U.S. is waiting for a "unified proposal." Separately, a former U.S. Treasury sanctions expert told Fox News that Iran may run out of oil storage capacity within two to three weeks and faces an estimated $435 million in daily economic losses from sanctions and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, raising the risk of fuel shortages and renewed domestic unrest.

The stalled second round of U.S.-Iran talks in Islamabad has reinforced concerns that no single Iranian authority has the standing to make and enforce strategic commitments. Analysts note that in Iran's political system, authority is partly performative — requiring visible, public leadership signals that are currently absent.

stories.headline: Kosovo Court Sentences Three Serbs to Life or Decades in Prison for 2023 Monastery Siege and Police Killing
stories.summary:
A court in Kosovo's capital Pristina on Friday sentenced two Kosovo Serbs to life imprisonment and a third to 30 years in prison for their roles in the September 2023 Banjska incident, in which an armed group of Kosovo Serbs ambushed police, killed an officer, and barricaded themselves inside a 14th-century Serbian Orthodox monastery. The court found the men guilty of violating Kosovo's constitutional order and inciting terrorist activities. Prosecutors have charged 45 people in total, but most are believed to be in Serbia and are unlikely to be extradited.

The attack began when police responded to a lorry blockade on a bridge in the early hours of September 24, 2023. An armed group of roughly 30 men attacked officers with guns and grenades, killing Sgt. Afrim Bunjaku before retreating to the Banjska monastery, where they held out through the day before slipping away when Kosovo special forces moved in. Three members of the armed group also died in the fighting.

Kosovo's acting President Albulena Haxhiu welcomed the verdict as proof that attacks on the state's constitutional order would not go unpunished. Kosovo authorities have long argued that Serbia's government bore ultimate responsibility for the attack, pointing to the self-confessed leader of the group, Kosovo Serb politician Milan Radoičić, who was the deputy leader of Serbian List, a party with close ties to Serbian President Aleksandar Vučić. Radoičić admitted to organizing the attack but faces no charges in Serbia and remains free there, though an Interpol arrest warrant limits his international travel.

The case has broad implications for Serbia-Kosovo relations and regional stability in the western Balkans. Belgrade denies official involvement, but Kosovo's government has used Radoičić's political connections to press its case internationally. The sentencing marks one of the most significant judicial outcomes from the incident, though the acquittal or absence of dozens of accused leaves the full picture of responsibility unresolved.

stories.headline: Supreme Court Rules 6-3 Military Contractors Can Be Sued Over Unauthorized Combat Conduct
stories.summary:
The Supreme Court issued a 6-3 ruling Wednesday allowing a lawsuit by U.S. Army veteran Winston Tyler Hencely to proceed against military contractor Fluor Corporation. Hencely suffered a fractured skull and permanent brain injuries in 2016 when a Taliban operative employed by Fluor detonated a suicide vest at Bagram Airfield in Afghanistan after Hencely confronted him.

Justice Clarence Thomas authored the majority opinion, joined by an unusual cross-ideological coalition: Justices Sotomayor, Kagan, Gorsuch, Barrett, and Jackson. The court rejected a broad "battlefield preemption" doctrine that would have shielded contractors from state-law tort claims in combat zones, finding that such immunity does not apply when the contractor's conduct was not authorized by the military and in fact violated military instructions.

The decision vacates a Fourth Circuit ruling that had dismissed Hencely's negligent retention and supervision claims, remanding the case for further proceedings. Chief Justice Roberts, along with Justices Alito and Kavanaugh, dissented.

The ruling carries significant implications for the defense contracting industry, which employs tens of thousands of personnel in conflict zones. Supporters of the decision argue it holds private companies accountable when they act outside the scope of military authorization; critics in the dissent warned the ruling could expose contractors to unpredictable state-law liability and complicate military operations.

stories.headline: Iran's Leadership Vacuum Deepens as New Supreme Leader Remains Out of Public View
stories.summary:
Questions about who is actually governing Iran are intensifying weeks into its war with the United States and Israel. Mojtaba Khamenei formally assumed the role of supreme leader after his father Ali Khamenei was killed on the first day of the conflict on February 28, but he has not made a public appearance since taking power, with his communications limited to a handful of written statements.

The New York Times, citing Iranian sources, reported this week that Mojtaba Khamenei may have sustained multiple injuries in the opening strikes, including facial injuries that have impaired his ability to speak. His absence has created what analysts describe as a vacuum of authority, with Iran's political system traditionally relying on the supreme leader's visible, performative presence to signal strategic direction.

On the diplomatic front, Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi continues to represent Tehran in U.S. talks, but a stalled second round of negotiations in Islamabad and a brief, quickly-retracted statement suggesting the Strait of Hormuz had reopened have underscored how little the diplomatic track controls military decisions. A message distributed by Iranian officials to citizens on mobile phones Thursday declared "there is no such thing as a hardliner or moderate in Iran — there was just one nation, one course," signaling an effort to project unity.

President Trump has publicly characterized Iran's leadership as "fractured" and said Washington is waiting for Tehran to produce a "unified proposal." The leadership uncertainty comes as the U.S. maintains what it describes as effective control of the Strait of Hormuz and economic pressure on Iran continues to mount.

stories.headline: EU Anti-Fraud Office Opens Formal Investigation into Former UK Ambassador Mandelson Over Epstein Ties
stories.summary:
The European Union's anti-fraud office, OLAF, has launched a formal investigation into Lord Peter Mandelson, the former UK ambassador to the United States who was dismissed in September 2025 after the extent of his relationship with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein became public. The European Commission requested the probe two months ago, covering Mandelson's four-year tenure as EU trade commissioner from 2004 to 2008.

Among the specific allegations under scrutiny are claims that Mandelson gave Epstein advance notice of an impending €500 billion eurozone bailout in 2010, when EU governments decided to intervene to prevent Greece's financial crisis from spreading. OLAF confirmed it now had "sufficient information" to proceed but said it was too early to determine whether the inquiry involved fraud specifically.

OLAF has no prosecutorial authority but can recommend sanctions, including judicial, financial, administrative, and disciplinary measures — potentially including revocation of Mandelson's pension from his time as commissioner. If criminal elements emerge, the office said it would refer matters to the European Prosecutor's Office. The UK's Metropolitan Police separately opened a criminal investigation earlier this year into allegations Mandelson passed market-sensitive information during his time as UK business secretary in 2009.

Mandelson has not publicly commented on the OLAF investigation, though the BBC reports he has denied acting criminally or being motivated by financial gain. The scandal continues to reverberate through British politics; earlier this week, former senior Foreign Office official Sir Olly Robbins said he had been pressured to expedite Mandelson's appointment as ambassador to the United States.

stories.headline: Supreme Court Rules Military Contractors Can Be Sued Over Unauthorized Combat Conduct
stories.summary:
The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 Wednesday that military contractors are not automatically shielded from civil liability for actions in war zones when their conduct was not authorized by the U.S. military. The majority opinion, written by Justice Clarence Thomas and joined by an unusual cross-ideological coalition including Justices Sotomayor, Kagan, Gorsuch, Barrett, and Jackson, vacated a Fourth Circuit ruling that had dismissed a lawsuit brought by Army veteran Winston Tyler Hencely.

Hencely suffered a fractured skull and permanent brain injuries in 2016 when a Taliban operative employed by contractor Fluor Corporation detonated a suicide vest at Bagram Airfield in Afghanistan. The court found that Fluor's conduct in retaining and supervising the attacker violated military instructions and therefore could not be shielded under a broad 'battlefield preemption' doctrine.

The decision rejects the argument that all contractor conduct in combat zones is immune from state-law tort claims, holding instead that the key question is whether the military authorized the specific conduct at issue. Chief Justice Roberts, Justice Alito, and Justice Kavanaugh dissented, suggesting concern about the breadth of litigation this ruling could invite against defense contractors operating in conflict zones.

The case now returns to lower courts for further proceedings. The ruling has significant implications for the defense contracting industry, which employs tens of thousands of personnel in support of U.S. military operations worldwide.

stories.headline: DOJ Drops Criminal Probe Into Fed Chair Jerome Powell, Refers Matter to Inspector General
stories.summary:
The U.S. Justice Department announced it is dropping its criminal investigation into Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell over alleged cost overruns in the renovation of Fed buildings, instead referring the matter to the Fed's internal inspector general. U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro announced the shift, with the White House framing the move as allowing the inspector general's 'more powerful authorities' to examine what it called the Fed's 'fiscal mismanagement.'

Powell had publicly disclosed in January that the DOJ had served the Fed with subpoenas and threatened a criminal indictment related to his Senate testimony about the renovations. He called the investigation 'unprecedented' and said it was tied to President Trump's anger over the Fed's refusal to cut interest rates, warning that central bank independence was at stake. Trump said at the time he knew nothing about the investigation.

The decision to drop the probe comes amid Senate consideration of Trump's nominee to replace Powell, Kevin Warsh. Senator Thom Tillis, a key Republican on the Senate Banking Committee, had withheld support for Warsh unless the administration dropped its probe of Powell — suggesting the move may be linked to securing confirmation votes. Powell's term expires May 15.

The episode has drawn broad attention as an example of the friction between the Trump White House and the Fed over monetary policy. Critics argued the criminal investigation was political intimidation; supporters of the inquiry said it represented legitimate oversight of billions in taxpayer-funded construction costs.

stories.headline: Tornado Strikes Enid, Oklahoma, Destroying Homes and Forcing Air Force Base Closure
stories.summary:
A powerful tornado struck Enid, Oklahoma on Thursday, tearing roofs from buildings, leveling homes in at least one neighborhood, knocking down utility poles, and forcing Vance Air Force Base to close until further notice. The confirmed tornado moved across Garfield County near Oklahoma's northern border, affecting a city of roughly 50,000 residents. Oklahoma emergency management officials reported 10 to 15 injuries as of Friday morning, with no deaths confirmed.

Among the hardest-hit areas was the Gray Ridge neighborhood on Enid's south side, where multiple homes were knocked completely off their foundations. Commercial buildings south of the city were reduced to twisted metal and splintered wood. Some residents were trapped by debris and required rescue by local police, the Enid Fire Department, and the Oklahoma Highway Patrol.

Vance Air Force Base, located in Enid, announced it was closed due to ongoing power and water restoration efforts, directing only mission-essential personnel to report. All base personnel were accounted for and no military injuries were reported. Portions of U.S. Interstate 81 and several local roads were closed due to storm damage.

Enid Mayor David Mason praised the community response, noting local businesses and residents had immediately mobilized to offer equipment, labor, and supplies to those affected. The National Weather Service confirmed the tornado's track, and damage assessments were continuing Friday.

stories.headline: Update: Trump Administration in Talks for $500 Million Spirit Airlines Bailout, Critics Warn Against Government Intervention
stories.summary:
The Trump administration is reportedly negotiating a roughly $500 million rescue package for Spirit Airlines that could leave the federal government owning up to 90 percent of the bankrupt carrier. Spirit has filed for bankruptcy twice and has been unable to stabilize its finances amid intense low-cost competition and rising operational costs.

President Trump floated the possibility of a bailout in recent remarks, signaling the administration's willingness to intervene in the struggling airline's fate. The deal, if finalized, would mark one of the more significant instances of direct federal equity ownership in a private company in recent years.

Critics across the political spectrum have pushed back. Millionaire investor Kevin O'Leary called the plan a "really bad idea," arguing that capitalism depends on failing businesses being allowed to fail and warning against rewarding what he described as bad management. Washington Examiner economics columnist Tiana Lowe Doescher similarly argued there is no good way to rescue Spirit without problematic government intervention.

The debate highlights a tension within conservative economic philosophy, as a Republican administration considers a substantial nationalization-style stake in a private airline. No final deal has been announced, and terms remain under negotiation.

stories.headline: New York Democrats Launch Ad Targeting GOP Gubernatorial Candidate Blakeman Over Trump Ties, Health Care
stories.summary:
New York Democrats released a new digital ad Friday attacking Nassau County Executive Bruce Blakeman, the Republican candidate for governor, over his ties to President Trump and his stance on health care. The 15-second spot, titled "Won't Change," signals an early and aggressive Democratic effort to nationalize the November gubernatorial race.

Blakeman is seeking to unseat incumbent Democratic Gov. Kathy Hochul. Democrats are betting that associating Blakeman with Trump and health care cuts will resonate in a state where Trump remains broadly unpopular in major population centers.

Republicans have not yet responded publicly to the specific ad, but the broader national context includes ongoing GOP debates over Medicaid and federal spending cuts tied to the reconciliation process, which Democrats are seeking to leverage at the state level as well.

The New York governor's race is considered one of the more competitive statewide contests of the 2026 cycle, with Republicans hoping that concerns over crime and cost of living could make the state more competitive than in recent cycles.

stories.headline: FCC Adds Foreign-Made Routers to National Security Covered List, Raising Questions About Future Software Support
stories.summary:
The Federal Communications Commission has updated its "Covered List" to include routers produced in foreign countries, blocking new models of that equipment from receiving FCC approval for sale in the United States. The move is framed as a supply chain national security measure, with particular concern directed at Chinese-manufactured networking equipment.

The rule does not immediately affect routers already owned by consumers or models previously approved for sale, meaning no existing devices are banned or disabled. However, the FCC simultaneously issued a temporary waiver allowing existing covered routers to continue receiving security and firmware updates through at least March 1, 2027, with a commitment to re-evaluate the policy before that date.

Cybersecurity experts note the longer-term concern: if update support is curtailed or not extended after 2027, home and business routers could become increasingly vulnerable to exploitation of known flaws. The FCC acknowledged this risk in its waiver language, noting that continued updates are essential to consumer protection.

The policy adds to a broader U.S. government effort to limit the presence of Chinese and other foreign-manufactured technology in critical communications infrastructure, following prior actions targeting Huawei, ZTE, and other firms.

stories.headline: 2026 NFL Draft Opens in Pittsburgh With Shocking Rams QB Pick, ESPN Coverage Draws Criticism
stories.summary:
Pittsburgh hosted the first round of the 2026 NFL Draft on Thursday night, marking the city's first time holding the event in nearly 80 years. Large crowds gathered at Acrisure Stadium as teams made their selections in what organizers described as a faster-paced format than previous years.

The Los Angeles Rams delivered the night's biggest surprise, staying at pick No. 13 to select Alabama quarterback Ty Simpson — a move that appeared to catch even head coach Sean McVay off guard. McVay was notably subdued at the post-pick press conference, and Simpson acknowledged he had never met McVay prior to being drafted. The New York Giants selected offensive lineman Francis Mauigoa at No. 10 overall, with Mauigoa pledging fierce loyalty to starting quarterback Jaxson Dart.

The Dallas Cowboys used their pick to address a defense that struggled badly in the previous season. Pittsburgh residents and local officials expressed pride in hosting the draft, citing the city's storied sports culture and its history of championship franchises across multiple leagues.

ESPN's broadcast coverage drew sharp criticism from viewers and commentators who said the network fell behind on announcing picks, aired excessive commercials, and failed to keep pace with the draft's accelerated format — a logistical challenge the league had promoted as an improvement to the event.

stories.headline: Ex-Michigan Coach Sherrone Moore's Former Mistress Reveals Pregnancy in ABC News Interview
stories.summary:
Paige Shiver, the former University of Michigan employee at the center of a scandal that led to the firing of head football coach Sherrone Moore, revealed in an emotional ABC News interview on Friday that Moore got her pregnant during their relationship. Shiver said she was subsequently diagnosed with Pompe disease, a condition causing muscle weakness and respiratory complications, and that multiple doctors advised her the pregnancy posed serious health risks.

Shiver recounted the night Moore was terminated by the university, saying he forced his way into her apartment shortly after being fired, stood close to her and said, 'You ruined my life,' then picked up butter knives and threatened to harm himself. She said she feared for her safety given the confrontation. Moore, who is 6 feet 4 inches tall, was later arrested on charges of stalking, breaking and entering, and home invasion.

Moore ultimately reached a plea deal in March 2026, pleading no contest to two misdemeanors — trespassing and malicious use of a telecommunications device — in exchange for the felony charges being dropped. The deal was finalized the same day a judge was set to hear a challenge to Moore's original arrest.

Michigan fired Moore in December 2025 after the relationship with Shiver became public. The case has drawn widespread attention to workplace relationships in college athletics and the university's handling of the situation.

stories.headline: New Details Emerge in Nigeria's Alleged 2025 Independence Day Coup Plot, Naming Alleged Mastermind
stories.summary:
Court documents filed by Nigerian state prosecutors have revealed new details about an alleged coup attempt that was thwarted on October 1, 2025 — Nigeria's Independence Day — including the name of the alleged chief strategist. The six civilians charged in the case, which includes a serving police inspector and retired military personnel, appeared in Federal High Court in Abuja this week, pleading not guilty to 13 counts including treason, terrorism, and money laundering.

Prosecutors allege the plot was masterminded by Colonel Mohammed Ma'aji, a 50-year-old officer with a background in security operations in Nigeria's Niger Delta region. Court documents also implicate Timipre Sylva, a former governor of Bayelsa state and one-time oil businessman, though Sylva was not formally indicted. The alleged plotters are said to have planned to destabilize the Nigerian state ahead of a takeover timed to coincide with a presidential parade that was abruptly cancelled at the last minute.

The Nigerian military acknowledged the coup attempt in January 2026, saying 16 unnamed senior officers would face court-martial, but provided few details at the time. The new civilian trial is expected to shed further light on the alleged conspiracy, which prosecutors say involved coordination across multiple branches of Nigeria's security services.

Nigeria has been under civilian rule since 1999 but has a lengthy history of military coups. Several neighboring West African nations have experienced coups in recent years, fueling speculation about Nigeria's political stability amid rising economic hardship and accusations of elite capture of the political system.

stories.headline: Netanyahu Discloses Early-Stage Prostate Cancer Treatment, Says He Is Cancer-Free
stories.summary:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu revealed Friday that doctors discovered and removed a small malignant tumor during routine monitoring following surgery for an enlarged benign prostate in 2024. Netanyahu, 76, said the treatment "removed the problem and left no trace of it" and that he is in "excellent physical condition."

Netanyahu said he deliberately delayed public disclosure to prevent Tehran from using the information for propaganda during the height of the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran. He said the tumor was "a tiny spot of less than a centimetre" and described his decision to treat it promptly as consistent with his broader approach to addressing dangers early.

The disclosure comes as Netanyahu is preparing for a visit to the White House in the coming weeks, where the U.S. is seeking to broker a lasting peace agreement following the Iran conflict. Israel's ceasefire with Lebanon, reached after fighting with Iran-backed Hezbollah, has also been extended by three weeks.

The revelation has drawn attention both to Netanyahu's health and to his management of the timing of the announcement. Israeli media and political observers noted the sensitivity of releasing such information during an active military conflict, while supporters said his swift treatment and transparency demonstrated responsible leadership.

stories.headline: DeSantis Launches Three-Part Strategy to Circumvent Florida's Anti-Gerrymandering Law and Create More GOP House Seats
stories.summary:
Florida Governor Ron DeSantis has quietly initiated a multi-tiered effort to evade the state's constitutional ban on partisan gerrymandering and redraw U.S. House district maps in ways that favor Republicans, according to reporting by Axios. DeSantis has called a special session of the state legislature for next Tuesday in Tallahassee to consider new congressional maps.

Florida law explicitly prohibits lawmakers from intentionally drawing congressional districts to give one party a political advantage. Critics say DeSantis's proposed approach is designed to sidestep that prohibition through procedural maneuvers, though the governor's office argues its redistricting goals are legally defensible and tied to broader nationwide redistricting activity.

The stakes are significant: partisan control of the U.S. House of Representatives could hinge on how Florida's congressional map is redrawn. Republicans currently hold a narrow House majority, and favorable Florida districts could prove pivotal in the 2026 midterm elections.

Democrats and redistricting reform advocates are expected to challenge any new map in court, arguing it violates the state's Fair Districts Amendment passed by voters in 2010. Republicans counter that the current maps underrepresent GOP voters and that a redraw is legally and politically justified given the national redistricting landscape.

stories.headline: Kenyan President Ruto Sparks African Diplomatic Uproar With Mockery of Nigerian English
stories.summary:
Kenyan President William Ruto has ignited a wave of online condemnation across Africa after publicly mocking Nigerian-accented English at a diaspora gathering in Italy earlier this week. Ruto told Kenyans in attendance that when Nigerians speak English, "you don't know what they are saying — you need a translator," while boasting that Kenyans speak "some of the best English in the world."

The remarks triggered fierce backlash from Nigerian officials, journalists, and public figures, who accused Ruto of colonial-minded thinking and demeaning a fellow African nation. Former Nigerian senator Shehu Sani noted on X that Nigeria is home to Nobel Prize-winning author Wole Soyinka as well as globally acclaimed writers Chinua Achebe and Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie.

Critics across the continent argued that judging nations by proximity to colonial English standards reflects a "deep inferiority complex rooted in colonial conditioning," as one widely shared post put it. Zimbabwean journalist Hopewell Chin'ono wrote that "English is a colonial language, not a measure of intelligence, capability, or national progress."

The incident follows a reciprocal pattern of online friction between Kenya and Nigeria, which escalated earlier this month when Nigerian President Bola Tinubu claimed Nigerians were "better off" than Kenyans despite domestic economic pressures. Both nations are major African economies and former British colonies with distinct English dialects shaped by hundreds of indigenous languages.

stories.headline: Supreme Court Rules Military Contractors Can Face Lawsuits Over Unauthorized Combat Conduct
stories.summary:
The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 Wednesday that military contractors are not automatically shielded from civil liability for conduct that was not authorized by the U.S. military, even when that conduct occurs in a war zone. The case was brought by Winston Tyler Hencely, a former Army specialist who suffered a fractured skull and permanent brain injuries when a Taliban operative employed by contractor Fluor Corporation detonated a suicide vest at Bagram Airfield in Afghanistan in 2016. The majority opinion, written by Justice Clarence Thomas, vacated a Fourth Circuit ruling that had dismissed the suit.

The decision produced an unusual ideological alignment: Thomas was joined by the court's three liberal justices — Sonia Sotomayor, Elena Kagan, and Ketanji Brown Jackson — as well as Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett. The majority rejected a broad 'battlefield preemption' doctrine that would have barred state-law tort claims tied to combat activities, holding instead that such preemption does not apply when a contractor's conduct violates explicit military instructions.

Chief Justice John Roberts, along with Justices Samuel Alito and Brett Kavanaugh, dissented. The dissenters had supported the broader preemption doctrine, which would have insulated contractors from liability for actions taken in combat zones regardless of authorization.

The ruling has significant implications for the defense contracting industry, which employs tens of thousands of personnel in active conflict zones. It opens the door for injured service members and others to pursue state-law negligence claims against contractors whose conduct was not sanctioned by the military, potentially reshaping legal accountability standards for the private sector in wartime operations.

stories.headline: UK Reasserts Falklands Sovereignty After Report of U.S. Pentagon Review of Position
stories.summary:
Downing Street issued a firm statement Wednesday reaffirming that sovereignty of the Falkland Islands 'rests with the UK,' after Reuters reported that an internal Pentagon email had discussed a potential U.S. review of its longstanding support for Britain's claim to the South Atlantic territory. The email, which the BBC has not independently reviewed and on which the Pentagon has not commented, was said to outline options for punishing NATO allies that did not support the U.S. war against Iran, with the Falklands review listed among possible measures alongside efforts to suspend Spain from NATO.

Prime Minister Keir Starmer's official spokesman said the government 'could not be clearer' about its position, citing the Falkland Islanders' overwhelming vote to remain a UK overseas territory and their right to self-determination. The spokesman said Britain had expressed this position 'previously clearly and consistently to successive US administrations and nothing is going to change that.' Spain's Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez separately downplayed the report, saying his government 'does not work based on emails.'

The Falkland Islands have been a British overseas territory since the 1833 British re-occupation, though Argentina — which calls the islands the Malvinas — still claims sovereignty. A 1982 Argentine invasion triggered a 10-week war in which 649 Argentine, 255 British military personnel, and three Falkland Islanders were killed before Argentine forces surrendered.

The report surfaces three days before King Charles and Queen Camilla are scheduled to visit Washington and meet with President Trump, adding diplomatic sensitivity to an already strained U.S.-UK relationship. Trump has previously expressed dissatisfaction with Britain's level of support during the U.S. conflict with Iran, while Starmer has repeatedly stated the UK will not be drawn into broader hostilities.

stories.headline: Update: UK Biobank Boss Blames 'Few Bad Apples' as ICO Opens Inquiry Into China Data Listing Incident
stories.summary:
The head of UK Biobank, Professor Sir Rory Collins, said Thursday that 'a few bad apples' at approved academic institutions were responsible for the incident in which medical data belonging to 500,000 volunteers was listed for sale on Alibaba in China. The government confirmed that datasets containing de-identified information from three academic institutions had been posted on the Chinese e-commerce platform but said the listings were 'swiftly' removed before any purchase was made. The three institutions have been banned from Biobank's research platform.

Sir Rory told BBC Radio 4's Today programme that he was 'angry' and 'upset' about the breach, and that the organization had effectively put science on hold by suspending all access to its online research platform while additional controls are implemented. Technology Minister Ian Murray told Members of Parliament that the data did not include names, addresses, or contact details, but could include gender, age, birth month and year, socioeconomic status, lifestyle habits, and biological sample measurements. Sir Rory acknowledged it was 'impossible' to entirely rule out that individuals could be re-identified using the de-identified data in combination with other information.

UK Biobank referred itself to the Information Commissioner's Office, which confirmed Thursday it had been informed of the incident and was making inquiries. An ICO spokesperson noted that organizations have legal responsibilities under data protection law regarding the handling of sensitive medical information.

The Biobank has collected intimate health data — including whole-body scans, DNA sequences, and medical records — from hundreds of thousands of volunteers over more than two decades. Its platform is used by scientists at approved academic institutions worldwide to conduct research on conditions including dementia, cancer, and Parkinson's disease.

stories.headline: Poisoning Suspected in Deaths of 18 Wolves at Italian National Park in Days-Long Mass Kill
stories.summary:
Authorities in Italy suspect deliberate poisoning in the deaths of 18 wolves over the course of several days in the Abruzzo, Lazio, and Molise National Park in central Italy. After 10 wolf carcasses were discovered last week and traces of suspected poisoned bait were found nearby, an additional eight were found when park patrols were intensified. The local animal health research institute IZS confirmed the 'presence of pesticides for agriculture used in poisoned bait for animals' in 13 of the wolves it examined.

Environment Minister Gilberto Pichetto Fratin condemned the killings as 'horrendous and serious,' stating that wolf protection was 'crucial to the balance of our ecosystem.' Italy's Institute for Environmental Protection and Research warned that the poisonings likely jeopardized significant advances in animal conservation and posed broader risks to other protected wild species, domestic pets, and public safety. Park authorities said they were collaborating with local public prosecutors.

The incident comes amid rising tension in Europe over wolf populations, which have grown by roughly 35 percent to approximately 23,000 between 2016 and 2023, according to EU figures. That growth has coincided with increased attacks on livestock — estimated at 65,500 animals killed annually — and prompted the EU to downgrade the wolf's conservation status from 'strictly protected' to 'protected,' opening the way for a limited annual cull of 160 wolves in Italy starting in 2026.

Environmental group Legambiente called the deaths an 'unprecedented attack on protected wildlife' and warned that other species could come under similar threat, including the critically endangered Marsican brown bear, of which only about 50 remain in the same park.

stories.headline: Supreme Court Rules 6-3 That Military Contractors Can Face Lawsuits Over Unauthorized Combat Conduct
stories.summary:
The Supreme Court ruled Wednesday that a lawsuit brought by a U.S. Army veteran injured in a 2016 Taliban suicide bombing at Bagram Airfield in Afghanistan may proceed, vacating a lower court dismissal. The 6-3 majority opinion, written by Justice Clarence Thomas, rejected a broad "battlefield preemption" doctrine that would have automatically shielded military contractors from state-law liability for conduct in combat zones.

Thomas was joined by an unusual cross-ideological coalition — Justices Sonia Sotomayor, Elena Kagan, Neil Gorsuch, Amy Coney Barrett, and Ketanji Brown Jackson — in holding that contractors are not immune from liability when their conduct was neither authorized by the military nor in compliance with military instructions. The plaintiff, former Army Specialist Winston Tyler Hencely, suffered a fractured skull and permanent brain injuries when a Taliban operative employed by contractor Fluor Corporation detonated a suicide vest after being confronted by Hencely.

Chief Justice John Roberts, along with Justices Samuel Alito and Brett Kavanaugh, dissented. The ruling remands the case to the Fourth Circuit for further proceedings consistent with the majority opinion.

The decision has significant implications for the accountability of private military contractors operating in war zones, potentially opening the door to state-law tort claims in cases where contractor conduct was not sanctioned or was explicitly prohibited by the U.S. military.

stories.headline: NATO Says No Mechanism Exists to Expel or Suspend Members After Report of U.S. Threat Against Spain Over Iran War
stories.summary:
NATO declared Wednesday there is no provision in its founding treaty to suspend or expel member states, responding to a Reuters report that an internal Pentagon email had floated punitive measures against allies — including Spain — that have refused to support U.S. military operations against Iran. The email reportedly also suggested revisiting U.S. diplomatic backing for the United Kingdom's claim to the Falkland Islands as leverage against European allies deemed insufficiently cooperative.

Spain has denied use of its two U.S. military bases — Naval Station Rota and Morón Air Base — for strikes on Iran, and Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez dismissed the report, saying his government operates on official documents, not internal emails. Sanchez said Spain supports full cooperation with allies "but always within the framework of international law."

The episode reflects broader tensions within the alliance since the U.S. and Israel struck Iran in late February, triggering Iranian restrictions on shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. President Trump has repeatedly criticized NATO allies for not doing enough to support the campaign, and last month called the alliance a "one-way street."

The UK, which has allowed U.S. forces to use British bases and participated in missions against Iranian drone attacks, has nonetheless said deeper involvement in the war or the U.S. naval blockade is not in its national interest. France, the UK, and other European allies have said they would help keep the strait open only after a lasting ceasefire.

stories.headline: BBC Investigation: Epstein Housed Abuse Victims in London Flats as Metropolitan Police Declined to Investigate
stories.summary:
A BBC investigation published Wednesday reveals that Jeffrey Epstein rented at least four flats in the London borough of Kensington and Chelsea to house women he is accused of abusing, doing so in the years after UK police decided not to open a formal investigation into him. The findings, drawn from millions of pages of U.S. Department of Justice records released as part of the Epstein files, show six of the women housed in those properties have since come forward as victims.

Many of the women, brought from Russia and Eastern Europe, arrived in the UK after the Metropolitan Police declined to pursue Virginia Giuffre's 2015 allegation that she had been trafficked to London. Some were coerced into recruiting other victims and were regularly transported to Paris to visit Epstein, according to emails in the files. A second woman had complained to the Met by early 2020 that she had been abused by Epstein in the UK, though it is unclear whether that complaint was acted upon.

Human rights lawyer Tessa Gregory of Leigh Day told the BBC she was "staggered" that no UK police investigation had ever been launched, citing a positive legal obligation under Article 4 of the European Convention on Human Rights to investigate credible trafficking allegations. The Met said it had fulfilled its legal duties and followed "reasonable lines of inquiry," including interviewing Giuffre multiple times.

The investigation expands the known scope of Epstein's UK operation significantly, revealing more victims, established housing infrastructure, and cross-border transportation networks operating until his 2019 death in jail.

stories.headline: Syria Arrests Key Suspect in 2013 Tadamon Massacre, One of Civil War's Most Documented Atrocities
stories.summary:
Syrian authorities have arrested Amjad Youssef, identified as the primary perpetrator of the April 2013 Tadamon massacre in Damascus, Interior Minister Anas Khattab announced Wednesday. Youssef was detained in Hama province following what authorities described as a "well-executed" security operation.

The Tadamon killings came to wide international attention in 2022 when leaked video footage showed Syrian soldiers leading bound and blindfolded civilians to a pit before shooting them. Human Rights Watch, which investigated the site, documented evidence of 288 killings in the Tadamon area, with 41 confirmed victims in the April 2013 incident and 11 blindfolded individuals shown being shot at close range in the leaked video.

The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights published footage appearing to show Youssef in a police vehicle following his arrest, his face visibly bloodied. The arrest marks a significant accountability development under Syria's post-Assad government, which took power after rebels toppled Bashar al-Assad in December 2024.

Human Rights Watch senior researcher Hiba Zayadin said the case illustrates "the former Syrian government's callous disregard for people's lives," calling Tadamon "a huge crime scene" and part of a broader pattern of state violence and apparent war crimes during a conflict that killed more than half a million people over 13 years.

stories.headline: UK Biobank Suspends Research Platform After Medical Data on 500,000 Volunteers Listed for Sale in China
stories.summary:
UK Biobank has temporarily suspended all access to its online research platform after de-identified medical data belonging to approximately 500,000 participants was found listed for sale on Alibaba, the Chinese e-commerce platform, the UK government announced Thursday. Biobank chief Professor Sir Rory Collins said the data had been misappropriated by researchers at three academic institutions that had been granted access to the platform and that those institutions have now been banned.

Sir Rory told BBC Radio 4 he was "angry" and "upset" about the incident, attributing it to "a few bad apples" who took data off the platform and offered it for sale. The UK and Chinese governments cooperated to have the listings removed before any purchase was completed, he said. Biobank has referred itself to the UK's Information Commissioner's Office, which confirmed it is making inquiries.

Technology minister Ian Murray told parliament the compromised data did not include participants' names, addresses, or contact details, but may include gender, age, birth month and year, socioeconomic status, lifestyle information, and biological sample measurements. Sir Rory acknowledged it was "impossible" to entirely rule out that individuals could be re-identified by combining the de-identified data with other available information, though he said there was no evidence that had occurred.

Biobank has collected detailed health data — including whole-body scans, DNA sequences, and medical records — from hundreds of thousands of UK volunteers over more than two decades. The dataset has been used in research on dementia, certain cancers, and Parkinson's disease, and its suspension, even temporarily, halts scientific work dependent on the platform.

stories.headline: Update: U.S. Soldier Charged With Insider Trading After Betting $400,000 on Maduro Capture
stories.summary:
An active-duty U.S. Army Special Forces soldier stationed at Fort Bragg, North Carolina, has been charged with insider trading and related federal crimes after allegedly placing bets on a crypto-based prediction market using classified information about the operation to capture Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro. Gannon Ken Van Dyke won more than $409,000 on Polymarket by wagering on the timing and outcome of Operation Absolute Resolve, the night-time raid on January 3 that seized Maduro and his wife from their Caracas compound.

The Department of Justice alleges Van Dyke created a Polymarket account on or about December 26, 2025, and placed bets of more than $33,000 while in possession of classified nonpublic information about the operation. He faces charges of unlawful use of confidential government information for personal gain, theft of nonpublic government information, commodities fraud, wire fraud, and making an unlawful monetary transaction.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said service members are 'prohibited from using this highly sensitive information for personal financial gain,' while U.S. Attorney Jay Clayton emphasized that prediction markets are 'not a haven for using misappropriated confidential or classified information.' Polymarket said it identified the suspicious trading, referred the matter to the DOJ, and cooperated with the investigation.

The case marks one of the first federal prosecutions involving insider trading on a crypto-powered prediction market, raising new legal questions about how existing securities and national security laws apply to the rapidly growing prediction market industry. The indictment was unsealed Thursday.

stories.headline: Update: Singer D4vd Had 'Significant Amount' of Child Sex Abuse Images at Time of Arrest, Prosecutors Say
stories.summary:
Prosecutors in Los Angeles revealed in court Thursday that singer D4vd, whose real name is David Anthony Burke, possessed 'a significant amount' of child sexual abuse material on his phone and iCloud account at the time of his arrest, discovered through search warrants in the investigation into the death of 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez. Prosecutors allege Burke repeatedly sexually abused the teenager before killing and dismembering her, actions they say he undertook to protect his music career.

Burke has pleaded not guilty to murder and other charges, including continuous sexual abuse of a child and mutilation of remains. His attorneys have maintained they 'will vigorously defend David's innocence' and said they believe the 'evidence will show that David did not kill Celeste.' He is not currently facing a separate criminal charge related to the newly disclosed child sexual abuse material.

Rivas Hernandez's body was found dismembered in a black zipper bag in the front trunk of Burke's Tesla in September 2025 after police received a report of a foul odor from a tow yard where the vehicle had been taken. Prosecutors say she was last seen at Burke's Hollywood Hills home on April 3, 2025, and had been reported missing by her family.

Two additional court hearings are scheduled for next week. The case has drawn significant public attention due to Burke's rise to fame through viral TikTok hits and the months-long gap between the discovery of remains and his arrest.

stories.headline: Update: White House Memo Accuses Chinese Firms of 'Industrial-Scale' AI Theft Through Distillation Campaigns
stories.summary:
The White House has issued an internal memo warning that foreign entities, principally based in China, are conducting coordinated campaigns to steal U.S. artificial intelligence advances through a process called 'distillation,' in which thousands of accounts are used to systematically extract proprietary information from American AI models. Michael Kratsios, Director of Science and Technology Policy, wrote that the aim of these campaigns is to 'systematically undermine American research and development and access proprietary information.'

Kratsios outlined four areas of White House action to detect and halt the exploitation, though the memo did not detail specific punitive measures against foreign entities. A White House spokesperson declined to comment beyond the memo's contents. Major AI companies including OpenAI and Anthropic have previously reported dealing with such activity; Anthropic has specifically identified Chinese laboratories DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax as having carried out distillation attacks against its models.

China's U.S. Embassy pushed back, saying China's AI development is 'the result of its own dedication and effort as well as international cooperation that delivers mutual benefits,' and objected to what it called 'unjustified suppression of Chinese companies by the US.' The embassy's statement also described China as becoming 'the world's innovation lab.'

The memo escalates a long-running dispute over AI intellectual property and national security, coming as competition between U.S. and Chinese AI developers has intensified following the rapid global uptake of DeepSeek's models earlier this year.
